perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information. "Confidential Information" shall mean and include any information which relates to the financial and/or business operations of each party, including, but not limited to, information relating to each party's customers, products, processes, financial condition, employees, manufacturing techniques, experimental work and/or trade secrets. Said Confidential Information is deemed proprietary by the parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information. "Confidential Information" shall mean and include any information which relates to the financial and/or business operations of each party, including, but not limited to, information relating to each party's customers, products, processes, financial condition, employees, manufacturing techniques, experimental work and/or trade secrets. Said Confidential Information is explicitly understood not to be proprietary by the parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Confidential Information is 'explicitly understood not to be proprietary,' we directly contradict the common understanding and legal protections afforded to trade secrets and proprietary information. This creates ambiguity about the enforceability of the non-disclosure provisions.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By stating that confidential information is NOT proprietary, this directly undermines the legal basis for trade secret protection under California law, potentially rendering the confidentiality agreement unenforceable for trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that Confidential Information is 'explicitly understood not to be proprietary,' which contradicts the definition and protection afforded to trade secrets under California law. The provided snippet from California Code, CIV 3426.1, outlines the legal framework for trade secret protection. By negating the proprietary nature of confidential information, the changed text undermines the ability to claim trade secret status and potentially invalidates the confidentiality agreement regarding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees not to use the other's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the specific consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information shall be made only upon the prior written consent from an authorized representative of the party which disclosed such information (hereinafter the "Disclosing Party") or pursuant to subsequent agreement between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees not to use the other's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the specific consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information shall be made at the Receiving Party's discretion and in accordance with what they perceive to be fair business practices.
perturbation.explanation: The original text strictly limits the use of confidential information. The change allows the receiving party to use the information at their discretion, conflicting with standard confidentiality practices and potentially violating trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17000
perturbation.law_explanation: This section provides a broad definition of unfair competition as including any unlawful, unfair or fraudulent business act or practice. Using confidential information at the Receiving Party's discretion without regard to the Disclosing Party's rights constitutes unfair competition under California law and potentially violates this code.

This chapter may be cited as the Unfair Practices Act. (Added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Receiving Party to use confidential information at their discretion, which directly contradicts the original text's strict limitations. The Law Explanation states that this constitutes unfair competition under California law. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Unfair Practices Act within the California Business and Professions Code (BPC), which the Law Explanation suggests is being violated.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that from the date of receipt, the party receiving the Confidential Information (hereinafter the "Receiving Party") shall not disclose Confidential Information to any other person, firm, corporation or other entity or use it for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall not publish, divulge, communicate, or reveal any Confidential Information to any person, corporation, or other third party or to any of Receiving Party's employees who do not have a need to know such Confidential Information with respect to their job duties. The Receiving Party shall use the same degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information as it would with respect to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that from the date of receipt, the party receiving the Confidential Information (hereinafter the "Receiving Party") shall endeavor to avoid disclosing Confidential Information to any other person, firm, corporation or other entity or use it for its own benefit except as provided in this Agreement. The Receiving Party shall attempt to not publish, divulge, communicate, or reveal any Confidential Information to any person, corporation, or other third party or to any of Receiving Party's employees who do not have a need to know such Confidential Information with respect to their job duties. The Receiving Party shall use a reasonable degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not disclose' with 'shall endeavor to avoid disclosing' and 'shall use the same degree of care' with 'shall use a reasonable degree of care' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 1201(b)(20) defines 'Good faith' as meaning honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. By weakening the Receiving Party's obligations to protect the confidential information and reducing the standard of care, the modified text could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, a breach of contract and potentially illegal.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific actions to protect confidential information ('shall not disclose,' 'shall use the same degree of care'), while the changed contract weakens these obligations ('shall endeavor to avoid disclosing,' 'shall use a reasonable degree of care'). This shift introduces ambiguity and reduces the standard of care, potentially conflicting with the duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation states that weakening obligations could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, potentially breaching the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines key terms within the Commercial Code, and while it doesn't directly address 'endeavor to avoid' vs 'shall not', the general context establishes a legal framework where clarity and adherence to standards are expected, and a weakening of the obligation can be seen as a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information and copies and extracts thereof shall be promptly returned to Disclosing Party three years from the effective date of this Agreement, or at any time within thirty (30) days of receipt of a written request by the Disclosing Party for the return of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information and copies and extracts thereof shall be destroyed by the Receiving Party three years from the effective date of this Agreement, unless the Receiving Party deems the information still useful, in which case they may retain it indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the return of confidential information. The modified text allows the receiving party to destroy the information, or keep it indefinitely if deemed useful, contradicting the ownership rights of the disclosing party and potentially enabling misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute addresses the theft or unauthorized use of trade secrets. By allowing the Receiving Party to retain and use the confidential information indefinitely, even after the agreement's initial term, it could create a scenario where the Receiving Party is unlawfully appropriating the Disclosing Party's trade secrets, violating California Penal Code § 499c.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PEN 499c. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Penal Code - PEN PART 1. OF CRIMES AND PUNISHMENTS [25 - 680.4] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 13. OF CRIMES AGAINST PROPERTY [450 - 593g] ( Title 13 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Larceny [484 - 502.9] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 499c. (a) As used in this section: (1) “Access” means to approach, a way or means of approaching, nearing, admittance to, including to instruct, communicate with, store information in, or retrieve information from a computer system or computer network. (2) “Article” means any object, material, device, or substance or copy thereof, including any writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype, model, photograph, micro-organism, blueprint, map, or tangible representation of a computer program or information, including both human and computer readable information and information while in transit. (3) “Benefit” means gain or advantage, or anything regarded by the beneficiary as gain or advantage, including benefit to any other person or entity in whose welfare he or she is interested. (4) “Computer system” means a machine or collection of machines, one or more of which contain computer programs and information, that performs functions, including, but not limited to, logic, arithmetic, information storage and retrieval, communications, and control. (5) “Computer network” means an interconnection of two or more computer systems. (6) “Computer program” means an ordered set of instructions or statements, and related information that, when automatically executed in actual or modified form in a computer system, causes it to perform specified functions. (7) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph or other reproduction of an article, and any note, drawing or sketch made of or from an article. (8) “Representing” means describing, depicting, containing, constituting, reflecting or recording. (9) “Trade secret” means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process, that: (A) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to the public or to other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (B) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. (b) Every person is guilty of theft who, with intent to deprive or withhold the control of a trade secret from its owner, or with an intent to appropriate a trade secret to his or her own use or to the use of another, does any of the following: (1) Steals, takes, carries away, or uses without authorization, a trade secret. (2) Fraudulently appropriates any article representing a trade secret entrusted to him or her. (3) Having unlawfully obtained access to the article, without authority makes or causes to be made a copy of any article representing a trade secret. (4) Having obtained access to the article through a relationship of trust and confidence, without authority and in breach of the obligations created by that relationship, makes or causes to be made, directly from and in the presence of the article, a copy of any article representing a trade secret. (c) Every person who promises, offers or gives, or conspires to promise or offer to give, to any present or for
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Penal Code § 499c regarding trade secrets. The original text requires return of confidential information, aligning with the Disclosing Party's ownership rights. However, the changed text permits the Receiving Party to destroy or indefinitely retain the information if deemed useful, directly conflicting with the Disclosing Party's rights and potentially allowing for misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' and outlines theft-related offenses, including unauthorized use. The modified text could lead to the Receiving Party using the trade secret without authorization, essentially 'appropriating' it, which is a violation under PEN 499c(b).
